summary: A West African empire (c. 1464-1591) ruled from its capital at Gao on the Niger River, which supplanted the Mali Empire as the Sahel's dominant trading power and drew its name from its ruling ethnic group. Founded by Sunni Ali (Sonni Ali Ber), it reached its greatest territorial extent under Askia Muhammad I, who founded the Askia dynasty after overthrowing Sonni Ali's son at the Battle of Anfao. Its history is chronicled in Arabic-language ta'rikhs like the Tarikh al-fattash and Tarikh al-Sudan, and it controlled the trans-Saharan salt-gold trade through cities like Timbuktu, Djenne, and the salt mines of Taghaza. The empire collapsed after Moroccan forces under the eunuch general Judar Pasha, sent by Sultan Ahmad al-Mansur, defeated it at the 1591 Battle of Tondibi.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: The empire is most consistently identified through its capital at Gao on the Niger River and its status as the successor to the Mali Empire as the Sahel's dominant trading power in the 15th and 16th centuries. It derives its name from its ruling ethnic group, and its two most famous rulers, cited as the empire's identifying figures in giveaway lines, are its founder Sunni Ali and Askia Muhammad, under whom the empire reached its greatest extent.

examples: name this West African empire, ruled from Gao, which supplanted Mali.
Gao was the capital city of this empire, whose greatest leader was Askia Muhammad.

examples: This empire replaced the Mali Empire as the preeminent power in West Africa.

clues.clue: Its most famous/identifying rulers were its founder Sunni Ali and Askia Muhammad
examples: This empire's most famous rulers were Askia Muhammad and its founder, Sunni Ali.

examples: derives its name from its ruling ethnic group.
identify this empire centered on the Niger in Western Africa, which succeeded the Mali empire and had its capital at Gao.

works.name: Founding under Sunni Ali (Sonni Ali Ber)
works.description: The empire was founded by Sunni Ali (Sonni Ali Ber), who defeated the Dogon and Fulani peoples near the modern-day city of Bandiagara and expelled the Tuareg from Timbuktu, earning the epithet "merciless" for his treatment of the Fulbe people. He conquered the remnants of the crumbling Mali Empire and built the new empire's wealth on control of trade cities like Timbuktu, and questions credit him with a legendary ability to transform himself into a vulture and render his armies invisible. The Songhai ethnic group that came to rule the empire had already inhabited its future capital, Gao, since the earlier reign of a King Kossoi.

clues.clue: Founded the empire by defeating the Dogon and Fulani peoples near the modern-day city of Bandiagara
examples: This empire was founded by a man who defeated the Dogon and Fulani near the modern-day city of Bandiagara.
Its founder defeated the Dogon and Fulani and expelled the Tuareg from Timbuktu.

clues.clue: Earned the epithet "merciless" for his treatment of the Fulbe (Fulani) people
examples: The first ruler of this empire gained the epithet "merciless" for his treatment of the Fulbe tribe.

examples: Name this emperor, who claimed to be able to turn himself into a vulture and make his armies invisible. He founded an empire whose capital city was Gao.

examples: Name this empire whose first emperor was Sunni Ali, who conquered the remnants of the Mali Empire, and gained vast wealth through control of trade cities such as Timbuktu.

clues.clue: The ruling ethnic group had already inhabited the future capital, Gao, since the earlier reign of King Kossoi
examples: The ethnic group who ruled this empire first inhabited its capital city during the reign of King Kossoi.

works.name: Askia Muhammad I & the Height of the Empire
works.description: Askia Muhammad I (Muhammad Ture) seized the throne from Sunni Ali's son Sonni Baru at the Battle of Anfao, assisted by his brother Kanfari Omar, and founded the Askia dynasty in place of the Sonni dynasty. During his debt-ridden hajj to Mecca (whose account plagiarized the earlier record of Mansa Musa's pilgrimage), he waged campaigns against the Mossi kingdoms and conquered the Sultanate of Agadez, and he was advised on religious and occult matters by the scholar al-Maghili. He established qadis (judges) in every Songhai city, and the empire reached its greatest territorial extent under his reign; he is said to be buried in Gao beneath a mud-brick tomb with distinctive protruding wooden scaffolding giving it the appearance of a spiky pyramid.

clues.clue: Reached the empire's greatest territorial extent under his reign
examples: This empire reached its height under Askia Muhammad.
The Songhai empire reached its greatest extent under this leader.

clues.clue: Seized the throne from Sunni Ali's son Sonni Baru at the Battle of Anfao, assisted by his brother Kanfari Omar, founding the Askia dynasty
examples: That ruler of this empire was assisted by his brother Kanfari Omar, usurped the throne at the Battle of Anfao, and has a tomb whose protruding scaffolding gives it the appearance of a spiky pyramid.
This man took power in the Songhai Empire after winning the Battle of Anfao. This so-called "great" ruler founded a namesake dynasty that replaced the Sonni dynasty.

clues.clue: Buried beneath a mud-brick tomb in Gao whose protruding wooden scaffolding gives it the appearance of a spiky pyramid

works.name: Administration, Trade & Chronicles
works.description: The empire relied on a river fleet of boats called hikoi to manage the Niger, which combined with its cavalry to provide most of its military power. It controlled the trans-Saharan salt-gold trade, contesting the salt mines of Taghaza with Morocco (fought over by the ruler Dawud), and its history is chronicled in Arabic-language ta'rikhs, notably the Tarikh al-fattash (attributed to the scribe, supposedly 125-year-old, Mahmud Kati, and commissioned by Dawud) and the Tarikh al-Sudan (by al-Sadi). A surviving copy of the empire's history, dubbed "Manuscript C," was argued by the historian Nehemia Levtzion to have been forged by Shehu Amadu, and one such chronicle contains a prophecy declaring the founder of the Massina state to be the Mahdi.

clues.clue: Contested the salt mines of Taghaza with Morocco; the ruler Dawud fought over them, and they were later raided by the Moroccan general Judar Pasha
examples: That chronicle was commissioned by this empire's ruler Dawud, who fought over the salt mines of Taghaza with Morocco.
After the reign of this empire's King Dawud, the eunuch Judar Pasha raided its salt mines at Tagharza.

examples: A ruler of this empire who captured the city of Djenne persecuted the scholars of the University of Sankore.

works.name: Fall: The Moroccan Invasion & the Battle of Tondibi (1591)
works.description: Sultan Ahmad al-Mansur of Morocco, nicknamed al-Dhahabi ("the Golden") for the short-term gains of the campaign, ordered an invasion of the empire, preceded a decade earlier by the Moroccan conquest of the Tuwat and Gurara oases. The eunuch general Judar Pasha, whose forces drew manpower partly from captives taken at the Battle of Alcacer Quibir, destroyed the salt mines at Taghaza before defeating the last Songhai ruler Askia Ishaq II at the decisive 1591 Battle of Tondibi, where the sound of Moroccan cannons and arquebuses turned back a Songhai cattle stampede meant to break the Moroccan lines. Victorious Moroccan military elites intermarried and settled to form the Arma ethnic group (named from the Arabic word for "fusilier"), and after the collapse a prince of the last royal Askia dynasty, Askia Nuhu, founded the successor Dendi Kingdom.

clues.clue: Songhai forces, led by the last ruler Askia Ishaq II, were defeated by Moroccan forces at the decisive 1591 Battle of Tondibi
examples: This empire's last ruler Askia Ishaq was forced out of power by that loss at the Battle of Tondibi.
Forces of this empire were lead by Ishaq II when it lost to Moroccan forces at Tondibi.

comprehensive_summary: The Songhai Empire (c. 1464-1591) was a West African empire ruled from its capital at Gao on the Niger River, deriving its name from its ruling ethnic group and succeeding the Mali Empire as the dominant power and trade empire of the Sahel. It was founded by Sunni Ali (Sonni Ali Ber), who defeated the Dogon and Fulani peoples near the modern-day city of Bandiagara, expelled the Tuareg from Timbuktu, and earned the epithet "merciless" for his treatment of the Fulbe people; he conquered the remnants of the crumbling Mali Empire and built the new empire's wealth on control of trade cities like Timbuktu, and was legendarily said to be able to transform into a vulture and render his armies invisible. The Songhai ethnic group had already inhabited Gao since the earlier reign of a King Kossoi, before the imperial expansion began. Sunni Ali's dynasty was overthrown by Askia Muhammad I (Muhammad Ture), who seized the throne from Sonni Baru at the Battle of Anfao with the help of his brother Kanfari Omar and founded the Askia dynasty. Askia Muhammad's debt-ridden hajj to Mecca, whose account plagiarized the earlier record of Mansa Musa's pilgrimage, was accompanied by campaigns against the Mossi kingdoms and the conquest of the Sultanate of Agadez; he was advised on religious and occult matters by the scholar al-Maghili (in a set of writings called "al-Maghili's Answers"), established qadis (judges) in every Songhai city, and saw his daughter Awah marry Muhammad Rumfa, making him an ancestor of the sultans of Kano. The empire reached its greatest territorial extent under his reign, and he is said to be buried in Gao beneath a mud-brick tomb whose protruding wooden scaffolding gives it the appearance of a spiky pyramid. Administratively, the empire relied on a river fleet of boats called hikoi to patrol the Niger, combined with cavalry, for most of its military power, and it controlled the trans-Saharan salt-gold trade, contesting the salt mines of Taghaza with Morocco — fought over by the ruler Dawud. Its history is chronicled in Arabic-language ta'rikhs, notably the Tarikh al-fattash (attributed to the scribe, supposedly 125-year-old, Mahmud Kati, and commissioned by Dawud) and the Tarikh al-Sudan (by al-Sadi); a surviving copy of the empire's history, dubbed "Manuscript C," was argued by historian Nehemia Levtzion to have been forged by Shehu Amadu, and one chronicle contains a prophecy declaring the founder of the Massina state to be the Mahdi. A Songhai ruler who captured the city of Djenne persecuted the scholars of the University of Sankore, and the scholar Ahmad Baba was later forced into exile during the Moroccan invasion. That invasion was ordered by Sultan Ahmad al-Mansur of Morocco, nicknamed al-Dhahabi ("the Golden") for the campaign's short-term gains, seeking control of the gold-salt trade; it was preceded a decade earlier by the Moroccan conquest of the Tuwat and Gurara oases. The eunuch general Judar Pasha, whose forces drew manpower partly from captives taken at the Battle of Alcacer Quibir, destroyed the salt mines at Taghaza — after preparations were hindered by a spy named Alfa Bubakar ben Lanbar — before defeating the last Songhai ruler, Askia Ishaq II, at the decisive 1591 Battle of Tondibi, where the sound of Moroccan cannons and arquebuses turned back a Songhai cattle stampede meant to break the Moroccan lines and sacked Djenne, Gao, and Timbuktu. Victorious Moroccan military elites intermarried and settled to form the Arma ethnic group, named from the Arabic word for "fusilier." The death of the empire's final ruler, in retribution for the death of a company of fusiliers, is chronicled in the Tarikh al-Fattash, which the invasion marked the endpoint of; the scholar Ahmed Babba al-Masufi lived through the collapse. After the fall, a prince of the last royal Askia dynasty, Askia Nuhu, founded the successor Dendi Kingdom.
recursive_suggestions: Battle of Tondibi — recurring independent answerline (3x as its own bonus answer, plus a separate 'invasion of the Songhai Empire' tossup answerline); has enough independent presence to eventually merit a standalone page
Askia Muhammad I — recurring individual ruler answerline (2x as own bonus answer); watch for growing independent presence
Sunni Ali (Sonni Ali Ber) — recurring individual founder answerline (1x own bonus part); currently folded into this page's founding section
Leo Africanus — recurring primary-source author answerline (2x own bonus/tossup-part answer) whose Description of Africa is cited as a key source on the empire; distinct topic
Timbuktu — recurring city answerline (1x own bonus part) central to the empire's trade and scholarship; likely its own Geography/History topic
Mali Empire — the predecessor empire that Songhai supplanted; distinct topic, mentioned here only for context

Excluded content: bonus parts whose actual answers were Gold, common salt/Taghaza, Askia dynasty (as generic term), Kano, Ahmad Baba al-Timbukti, Bambara Empire, Griots, Mossi, Fulani people, Battle of Alcacer Quibir — these are distinct answerlines only tangentially connected to Songhai and were not used as this topic's own clue content
